title: Justinian closes the Academy of Athens
blurb: The last Neoplatonist teachers scattered, some to Persia, carrying theurgic Neoplatonism out of the classical world and into the underground streams that would resurface in Islam and the Renaissance.
source: Gregory Shaw, Theurgy and the Soul

title: Isidore of Seville taxonomises magic
blurb: Book VIII of Isidore's Etymologiae lists the kinds of magician — magi, necromantii, augurs, haruspices — a catalogue medieval writers quarried for a thousand years.
mtg: An early attempt to sort magic into types, as our lexicon does.
source: Isidore, Etymologiae VIII; Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

title: Anglo-Saxon galdor and the metrical charms
blurb: Old English healing charms — the Nine Herbs Charm, Wið Færstice — mix Christian prayer, Germanic galdor (incantation), and herb-lore. Here the English 'spell' (a saying) is still doing magic aloud.
mtg: The homegrown English root of 'spell,' before it meant a card you cast.

title: Jābir ibn Ḥayyān (Geber) and Arabic alchemy
blurb: The vast Jabirian corpus gave alchemy its mercury–sulphur theory of metals and its dream of the elixir, and coined much of its technical Arabic — al-iksīr, al-kīmiyāʾ — that Latin Europe would inherit whole.
mtg: The Arabic engine room of the whole alchemy family of words.
source: Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy; William Newman on the Latin Geber

title: al-Kindī's De Radiis — a physics of magic
blurb: Al-Kindī theorised that everything radiates 'rays' that act at a distance; words, figures, and characters could focus them. It gave talismans and sigils a natural-philosophical mechanism.
source: al-Kindī, De Radiis; Vittoria Perrone Compagni (renaissance magic corpus)

title: Abū Maʿshar, prince of astrologers
blurb: Abū Maʿshar's great astrological syntheses, later translated into Latin, carried Aristotelian celestial causation and the technical apparatus of the horoscope into Christian Europe.
source: Paola Zambelli, Astrology and Magic (renaissance magic corpus)

title: Old English wicce / wicca attested
blurb: The feminine wicce and masculine wicca appear in Anglo-Saxon texts and law, alongside wiccian, 'to work magic' — one of the oldest and murkiest magic-words in English.

title: The Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm (Picatrix) compiled in al-Andalus
blurb: This Arabic compendium of astral magic — how to trap planetary spiritus in talismans by choosing the right hour and matter — is the single most important source for later European image-magic.
mtg: The talisman-maker's handbook; every 'Talisman' card is downstream of it.
source: Attrell & Porreca (trans.), Picatrix; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. picatrix

title: Old English wǣrloga — the oath-breaker
blurb: Warlock begins not as a male witch but as wǣrloga, 'oath-breaker, traitor' — a covenant-breaker, later 'one in league with the Devil.'

title: Norse runic magic and the rūn
blurb: Old English and Norse rūn means 'secret, mystery.' The runic letters were named for the hidden power that knowing them conferred; the Eddas credit Odin with winning them in agony.
mtg: An alphabet whose very name means 'secret.'

title: Michael Psellos on demons in Byzantium
blurb: The Byzantine polymath Psellos wrote a taxonomy of demons and preserved Chaldean and Neoplatonic magical lore, a bridge from late-antique theurgy to the medieval world.

title: The Sabians of Harran keep the star-cult
blurb: The Sabian community at Harran preserved Hermetic and planetary religion under Islam, transmitting star-worship and talismanic doctrine that fed straight into the Picatrix.
source: Kevin van Bladel, The Arabic Hermes
